dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond for shortness of breath in Richmond.
Good afternoon, sir. I'm Alex with EMS. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been having shortness of breath for three days, and it got worse this morning when I tried to go down the stairs.
You seem alert and oriented. Can you tell me your name and where you are?
Mr. Johnson, what is today's date, and can you tell me where you are again?
Airway is clear. You mentioned being upright. Are you more comfortable like this?
Breathing is 18 breaths per minute. You can speak between breaths. Are you experiencing any chest discomfort?
Radial pulse is strong and regular bilaterally. Any unusual sensations or dizziness?
Skin is warm, dry, with normal color. Cap refill is less than 2 seconds. Any rashes or swelling?
Breath sounds are bilateral, with some wheezing and rales. Any cough or sputum production?
You mention shortness of breath when moving. Do you find it easier to breathe when sitting upright?
SpO₂ in room air is 80%. I'll put you on 3 LPM oxygen via nasal cannula now.
No jugular venous distension observed at the moment. Neck is symmetrical.
No signs of abdominal pain or tenderness at this time. Any issues in your stomach or lower chest?
GCS is 15. You appear calm and cooperative. Any confusion or difficulty remembering?
Your history includes hypertension, CHF, old MI, and an AICD. Any other known conditions?
You're currently taking blood thinners and several others. You say you've been complying with regimens.
You mentioned a productive cough with white sputum and worsening this morning.
Initial pulse is 91, slightly elevated.
Respiratory rate remains at 18.
Blood pressure is 145 over 91.
Oxygen saturation is 92% with current support.
Glucose is 113. Blood sugar appears stable.
With oxygen on, your saturation has improved to 98%.
Recheck shows respirations at 36 now. Working a bit harder.
Blood pressure is now 147 over 92.
Glucose is not measurable but no hypoglycemic symptoms are reported.
I've set up nasal cannula at 3 LPM to support your oxygen needs.
Placed you on 4-lead monitoring for ongoing rhythm analysis.
12-lead ECG was taken and showed a possible fascicular block and LAE.
We attempted IV access in left AC with 20G, then in your right hand with 20G, but no success.
Reassessed GCS: 15. Fully alert, responding appropriately.
Pupils equal, round, reactive, 3mm in size.
Administering Duoneb with 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Ipratropium via neb mask with 8 LPM air flow.
Continuous etCO₂ monitoring is now active.
After Duoneb, your oxygen saturation is maintained at 100%.
No change in responsiveness: still GCS 15.
12-lead ECG re-run shows sinus rhythm. No acute ST changes noted.
You stated you felt some improvement after the nebulizer treatment. Any worsening symptoms?
Throughout the evaluation, you reported no additional pain beyond shortness of breath.
Final vitals: pulse 92, BP 147/88, 28 respirations. Stable and trending toward improvement.
No signs of tenderness, rigidity, or masses in your abdomen.
Chest wall is symmetrical with no deformities or tenderness. Expansion is equal bilaterally.
No swelling or edema in your extremities. Movement and strength are normal.
You will be secured in Fowler's position with lap and shoulder restraints for transport.
Destination is Chippenham Hospital, chest pain room 12 for evaluation.
Rechecking alertness: name, location, time?
You still appear to have some work of breathing, but no immediate distress.
Inquiries show no history or signs of recent falls.
We check again for jugular distention and find none.
Still no abdominal pain or rigidity detected.
No pain reported in the assessment for chest or abdomen.
Still no pain or discomfort in the stomach area.
No recent loss of consciousness or dizziness.
No chills or fever reported in the history.
You don't report any headache or visual changes.
12-lead showed mild widening of the QRS, consistent with a fascicular block.
You describe orthopnea, meaning you prefer to stay upright due to breathing difficulty.
No signs of trauma or recent falls.
Managed using Protocol 3-11: Respiratory Distress-COPD/Reactive Airway.
We have you secured for transport in a seated position for safe transfer.
Hospital destination reconfirmed: Chippenham Hospital, Emergency Department.
Updated assessment: shortness of breath worsened this morning with no chest pain.
No sign of recent trauma, loss of consciousness, or unconscious secondary events.
Breathing at 28 per minute after treatment remains in the tachypneic range.
Oxygen and nebulizer were administered as per the COPD/Respiratory Distress protocol.
Positioning is critical for symptom control, so we ensure you stay in the Fowler's position.
No signs of head trauma or neurological deficit observed.
You described an abrupt worsening in breathlessness this morning when walking, consistent with orthopnea.
Still stable and responsive, no signs of acute cardiac or pulmonary event.
Current saturation is 100%, which is excellent with the treatment.
Let me check: Mr. Johnson, can you squeeze my hand? Awareness is still present.
Patient shows full responsiveness throughout. No cardiac arrest event today.
Head and neck are normal, no swelling or trauma. Pupils reactive bilaterally.
Our care is guided by Protocol 3-11: Respiratory Distress for this patient.
All monitoring and lines are in place. Still stable for the trip to the hospital.
Medical history confirms: HTN, old MI, CHF, and AICD.
No signs of infection or additional clinical concern beyond respiratory distress.
Patient's condition is consistent for transport while maintaining current supportive care.
Skin remains normal with no unusual rashes or discolorations.
No hypoxia detected throughout. Oxygen support is effective.
Attempts for IV placement made: left AC and right hand, twice each-still not successful.
Notes are complete, including medications, EKG changes, and interventions.
Rechecked pulse, BP, respirations. All stable prior to moving to the unit.
Final vitals before departure-pulse 92, BP 147/88, respirations 28, SpO2 100.
Successful in administering Duoneb and providing adequate oxygen support.
EKG monitoring shows improvement in rhythm: no acute event detected.
Still able to take in full breaths and talk between words.
Final exam shows no new findings-everything stays consistent.
All checks completed. Movement assisted and safety verified.
Did not experience worsening pain, CPR, or need for extra interventions.
Take these vitals and our reports to the nurses. Any more questions?
Patient status: green for transport. All systems normal with supportive care.
Ambulance is prepared for departure, patient is ready for transfer.
Transport initiated to Chippenham Hospital-ongoing monitoring in-transit.
Time marked: 10:59 AM. Movement initiated. All systems normal for travel.
Repositioning for comfort and continued oxygen delivery during transport.
Reviewing actions: all in line with trained outcomes and assigned protocols.
Essentials rechecked-stable to move and arrive.
Transfer complete all set for ED placement.
